title: Spatial distribution of foot traffic in New York City and applications for urban planning
data_sources: NYC Motor Vehicle Collisions crash data
NYC Open Data POIs (schools, parks, subway)

data_scope.study_area: New York City, five boroughs, 59 Community Districts
data_scope.calibration_counts: NYC DOT observed pedestrian counts, 2018-2019 (exact station count not stated)

core_conclusion: The first city-wide calibrated foot-traffic model for New York City, built over a 315,577-segment sidewalk/crosswalk network for six peak periods, shows that many outer-borough streets carry footfall comparable to central Manhattan yet remain undercategorized for pedestrian priority, and that the highest exposure-adjusted pedestrian injury risk often lies outside Manhattan once crashes are normalized by modeled volume.
why_it_matters: Provides a continuous city-wide pedestrian-volume surface for the most common, least measured travel mode; reframes safety as exposure-adjusted risk; audits official street classifications to expose investment inequity; releases a reusable calibrated network and model files.
why_nature: City-scale methodological first; links pedestrian flow geometry to investment inequity and injury risk; directly policy-translatable to street reclassification, Vision Zero and capital budgeting; open reusable artifact (calibrated network + model files).
added_value_to_my_research: Learned closed-form gravity trip generation -> flow-learned distance decay for R2SFCA
Street-network structural invariants -> interpretable priors/null model for explainable spatial prediction
Passive mobility validation -> continuous footfall field via spatiotemporal KDE and data fusion
Foot-traffic surface as exposure denominator -> health and pollution exposure equity diagnostics
Scenario/agent-based intervention engine -> built-environment to physical-activity simulation
